items.q: Why do tertiary halogenoalkanes typically react via SN1 rather than SN2?
items.correct: The tertiary carbocation intermediate is stabilised by three alkyl groups (positive inductive effect), making the SN1 pathway favourable.
items.misconception: Tertiary halogenoalkanes react via SN1 because they are more reactive in every way.
items.random_wrong: Tertiary halogenoalkanes react via SN2 because they have more bonds to break.
items.why: Students mix up steric and electronic factors — they don't realise it's the STABILITY of the carbocation (electronic) plus STERIC HINDRANCE (blocking SN2) that drives the SN1 preference.

items.q: How does the rate of an SN2 reaction depend on concentrations?
items.correct: Rate is first order in both substrate AND nucleophile (overall second order) because both are involved in the rate-determining step.
items.misconception: SN2 reactions are first order overall because only the substrate matters.
items.random_wrong: SN2 reactions are zero order because they happen in one step.
items.why: Students confuse SN1 (rate depends only on substrate) and SN2 (rate depends on both); the '2' in SN2 refers to the order, but they forget which way round.

items.q: Why does benzene undergo electrophilic SUBSTITUTION but alkenes undergo electrophilic ADDITION?
items.correct: Substitution preserves benzene's stable delocalised pi system; addition would destroy aromaticity at a large energetic cost.
items.misconception: Benzene reacts the same way as alkenes — by addition across one of its double bonds.
items.random_wrong: Benzene cannot react with electrophiles at all.
items.why: Students see C=C in both structures and expect identical mechanisms, missing that delocalisation fundamentally changes the energetics.
It's Second order overall. “First order in R-X only” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.
It's SN1. “SN2” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Rate of SN2 doubles when [Nu⁻] doubles. Order with respect to nucleophile is:
items.why: The real figure is 1. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
